questions.q: Which register holds the address of the memory location currently being read from or written to?
questions.explain: The Memory Address Register (MAR) holds the address of the location to be accessed, whereas the MDR holds the data.

questions.q: During the fetch phase, which two actions are most commonly forgotten in an exam trace?
options: Copy PC to MAR and read the data bus
Increment the PC and copy MDR to CIR
Send a write signal and clear the ACC
questions.explain: After fetching, the PC is incremented and the contents of the MDR are copied into the CIR ready for decoding.

questions.q: A CPU's address bus is widened from 16 to 20 lines. What is the main effect?
options: Data can be transferred faster per cycle
More memory locations can be addressed
questions.explain: Address bus width determines the number of addressable locations (2^n), so 20 lines allows 2^20 locations.

questions.q: Which statement about the system buses is correct?
options: The address bus is unidirectional from CPU to memory
The control bus only carries the clock signal
questions.explain: The address bus is unidirectional (CPU to memory), while the data and control buses are bidirectional.

questions.q: Adding more cores will only make a program run faster if which condition is met?
options: The clock speed is also doubled
The task is parallelisable and the software supports it
The program is written in assembly
questions.explain: Extra cores only help when the task can be split into parallel parts and the software is written to exploit them.

questions.q: When the CPU needs data, in which order are the cache levels and RAM checked?
questions.explain: The CPU checks the fastest, smallest cache first (L1), then L2, then L3, and finally the much slower RAM.

questions.q: Why does a conditional jump (branch) instruction reduce the benefit of pipelining?
options: It writes a new address to the PC, so prefetched instructions may be flushed
It always doubles the clock speed
It converts the CPU to Harvard architecture
questions.explain: A branch writes a new address into the PC during execute, so instructions already fetched into the pipeline may be wrong and must be discarded.

questions.q: Which feature distinguishes Harvard architecture from Von Neumann architecture?
options: It uses a single shared bus for everything
It stores data and instructions in separate memories with separate buses
It cannot be used in embedded systems
It removes the need for a control unit
questions.explain: Harvard architecture uses separate memory and buses for data and instructions, allowing simultaneous fetch and data access.

questions.q: A smartphone chip uses few, simple, fixed-length instructions to keep power low. Which design is this?
questions.explain: RISC uses few simple fixed-length instructions, lower power and easier pipelining, making it suited to battery-powered ARM devices.

questions.q: Why is a GPU well suited to training neural networks but poor at branch-heavy sequential code?
options: It has a single very fast core
It has thousands of small cores performing the same operation on large data in parallel
It has no cache at all
It only works with CISC instructions
questions.explain: A GPU has thousands of small cores applying the same operation across large data sets (SIMD), but it handles dependency-laden sequential branching poorly.

questions.q: A hospital needs to archive huge volumes of records cheaply, with restores rarely needed. Which storage best fits?
questions.explain: Magnetic tape offers very low cost per GB and high capacity for serial archive, where slow random access is acceptable.

questions.q: Which statement correctly contrasts flash/SSD storage with magnetic HDD storage?
options: Flash has moving parts; HDD does not
Flash is volatile; HDD is non-volatile
Flash is faster, silent and durable but costs more per GB
HDD has a finite number of write cycles but flash does not
questions.explain: Solid-state flash has no moving parts so it is fast, silent and durable, but it costs more per GB than magnetic disks.

questions.q: Which pair of properties correctly describes RAM?
questions.explain: RAM is volatile (contents lost on power off) and can be both read from and written to.

questions.q: A user opens many large programs and the machine slows to a crawl with constant disk activity. What is happening?
options: Excessive paging between RAM and disk, known as disk thrashing
questions.explain: When RAM is full, virtual memory pages are swapped constantly to and from slow disk, causing disk thrashing.
